one_line_takeaway: By reframing LLM watermarks as a key-module + mark-module pair, the paper pins the imperceptibility-vs-robustness trade-off on key restoration and fixes it with WaterPool, a plug-in that stores per-generation keys in a vector DB and restores them by semantic retrieval, lifting efficacy, robustness, and imperceptibility of KGW/EXP/ITS at the cost of O(N) storage.
key_finding: WaterPool simultaneously restores near-optimal imperceptibility AND boosts efficacy/robustness of all three host watermarks, empirically loosening the trade-off. On OPT-1.3B, imperceptibility Distinct-N gaps shrink to ~0 (e.g. EXP's Glob-Distinct3 delta of -17.0 becomes -0.0 for EXP-Pool; ITS's -9.8 becomes -0.0). For robustness (TPR@FPR=1%), the headline gains on open-text-generation Translation-Attack are +12.73 for KGW (29.53 to 42.26), +20.27 for EXP (31.14 to 51.41), and +7.27 for ITS (3.56 to 10.83); ITS gains most under Lexical-Attack (+42.27, 26.23 to 68.50) and w/o attack (+19.12, 73.43 to 92.56). Retrieval-based key restoration stays stable as negatives grow to 1.8M and DB grows to ~100M items, unlike the naive retrieval-watermark baseline (Krishna et al.) which degrades >40% under many non-watermarked neighbors because it scores by raw similarity rather than by a key-seeded statistic.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is twofold. (1) A conceptual 'key-centered scheme' that decomposes any private LLM watermark into two independent modules connected only by a private key: a KEY MODULE (samples a private key at generation, restores it at detection) and a MARK MODULE (uses the key as a seed to reweight the next-token distribution and to compute a per-token statistic). Using this decomposition the authors argue, for the first time in their framing, that the widely-observed trade-off among imperceptibility, efficacy, and robustness is rooted in the key module: imperceptibility demands a large i.i.d. key sampling space (so watermarked text is indistinguishable even over many queries), but a large space makes key RESTORATION during detection hard, which hurts efficacy and robustness. Prior methods resolve this either by shrinking/fixing the key space (Unigram, EXP, ITS) at the cost of imperceptibility, or by context-hash key restoration (KGW, Gamma, Delta) at the cost of robustness to attacks. (2) WaterPool, a concrete new key module that keeps the full k~U(R^L) sampling space (preserving imperceptibility) while replacing fragile hashing / exhaustive greedy search with a SEMANTIC-RETRIEVAL key restoration: each generation stores (sentence-embedding(output), private_key) in a vector database, and at detection the key of the semantically most-similar stored output is retrieved. Because a watermarked text (even after attack) is closest to its own stored version, correct-key retrieval is near-guaranteed. WaterPool is a drop-in plug-in composed with three existing mark modules to yield KGW-Pool, EXP-Pool, and ITS-Pool.
threat_model: Watermarking is defended as a way to trace/attribute LLM-generated text to a specific model, motivated by misuse (mass fake news, automated social-media manipulation, academic-integrity and IP violations). The adversary possesses watermarked text and tries to strip the statistical watermark signal via semantics-preserving edits while keeping meaning intact. Three attack types are evaluated: Lexical-Attack (random add/delete/replace of a small portion of tokens), Dipper-Attack (a dedicated paraphrasing model from Krishna et al. 2023), and Translation-Attack (round-trip translation). The detector must retain a high true-positive rate at a fixed 1% false-positive rate under these attacks (robustness), keep false positives low against non-watermarked text from humans and other models (efficacy), and avoid perceptibly shifting the model's output distribution (imperceptibility / undetectability, formalized as N-shot undetectability from Hu et al. 2023).
limitations: The authors concede WaterPool does not eliminate the trade-off: it only fixes the key module, while the mark module remains a bottleneck (KGW-Pool cannot reach optimal imperceptibility because KGW's F is not unbiased, and even the improved absolute robustness under Dipper paraphrasing stays low, e.g. ITS-Pool ~4-6% TPR). It requires maintaining a per-generation vector database, giving O(N) storage that grows unbounded with usage (estimated ~520GB/month at ChatGPT scale) plus retrieval-time cost, so it is a server-side/stateful scheme rather than a stateless detector. Detection correctness hinges on the assumption that an attacked watermarked text remains its own nearest neighbor in embedding space; a strong enough paraphrase that moves it closer to another entry (or off-DB text) would break restoration. Evaluation is limited to OPT-1.3B/6.7B and short 50-70 token generations; no modern large chat models are tested. Better mark-module design is left to future work.

datasets_used: ELI5 / 'Explain Like I'm Five' (long-form question answering), ~3000 sampled prompts
20 outputs per prompt, ~60000 samples total for evaluation
Negative-sample sets: 3K human-written texts; 1.8M outputs from 6 other models (Gemma-2b/7b, Llama2-7b/13b, Vicuna-7b/13b)
quantitative_results: Robustness TPR@FPR=1%, OPT-1.3B, open-text-gen Translation-Attack: KGW 29.53 -> KGW-Pool 42.26 (+12.73); EXP 31.14 -> EXP-Pool 51.41 (+20.27); ITS 3.56 -> ITS-Pool 10.83 (+7.27)
ITS w/o attack (open gen): 73.43 -> 92.56 (+19.12); ITS Lexical-Attack: 26.23 -> 68.50 (+42.27)
LFQA Translation-Attack: EXP 54.99 -> EXP-Pool 72.61 (+17.62); ITS 8.19 -> ITS-Pool 24.26 (+16.07)
Imperceptibility (Glob-Distinct3 delta vs non-watermark, OPT-1.3B open gen): EXP -17.0 -> EXP-Pool -0.0; ITS -9.8 -> ITS-Pool -0.0; KGW -2.7 -> KGW-Pool +2.4
Perplexity stays ~7.8 (matching non-watermark) for EXP-Pool/ITS-Pool vs EXP 7.8/ITS 7.5 baseline; Unigram 9.9, KGW 9.6
Naive retrieval-watermark baseline degrades >40% worse than other methods when many non-watermarked neighbors are added
Cost: 128-dim bfloat16 embedding + int32 key = 260 bytes/item; O(N) storage ~520GB/month at 2B ChatGPT visits; retrieval <0.001 sec/item on 100M-item DB using 10 NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPUs
Most watermarks reach >90% TPR@FPR=1% without attack; robustness stable as DB scales 1e5 -> 1e8
baselines_compared: EXP (Kuditipudi et al. 2023, Gumbel-max) vs EXP-Pool
ITS (Kuditipudi et al. 2023, inverse-transform sampling) vs ITS-Pool
Gamma and Delta (Hu et al. 2023, unbiased watermarks)
Retrieval watermark (Krishna et al. 2023) as a conceptual contrast
